title: Expect 100-continue, Early Responses, Body Rejection, and Draining · Systems Transfer
summary: An independent protocol team receives different fragmentation, peer, trust, timing, and failure constraints and transfers expect 100-continue, early responses, body rejection, and draining into a new expectation and early-response coordination state machine. Planned artifact: Produce an independent changed-context version of expectation and early-response coordination state machine with automated and judgment evidence. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice…
objectives: Parse Expect, support 100-continue deliberately, and return appropriate final failure for unsupported expectations.
Send 100 Continue only after header-level policy accepts receiving content and before a final response commits.
Bound client waiting for 100, handle early final responses, and avoid sending content after rejection.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Commit to the mission model · Lab Expectation Grammar Policy
steps.instruction:
lab case — A lab protocol team handles case 7476 in a archival client parsing incomplete close-delimited responses. Build deterministic Go evidence to coordinate 100-continue and bounded early rejection without deadlock or unsafe reuse; preserve this operating constraint: diagnostics must omit credentials and attacker-controlled metric labels; then force a short write and retain the exact transmitted prefix plus remaining count. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Commit to the mission model · Lab Expectation Grammar Policy.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for parse expect, support 100-continue deliberately, and return appropriate final failure for unsupported expectations. For Commit to the mission model · Lab Expectation Grammar Policy, keep Expectation Grammar Policy connected to executable Go behavior evidence.

For test an acceptance boundary, run one convenient Expect Deadlock Proof happy path, omit ownership, error, changed input, goroutine stop, and full-toolchain checks, then accept plausible output. This does not satisfy Expect Deadlock Proof for expectation and early-response coordination state machine.
Reject the unsupported conclusion, name the violated Expect Deadlock Proof condition, repair the responsible layer, and rerun the boundary trace.

steps.title: Defend the transferred model · Lab Expect Deadlock Proof
steps.instruction:
Write the lab evidence note for Expect Deadlock Proof: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A lab protocol team handles case 7485 in a canary edge service advertising HTTP/3 with bounded fallback. Build deterministic Go evidence to coordinate 100-continue and bounded early rejection without deadlock or unsafe reuse; preserve this operating constraint: no malformed framing may be normalized and forwarded to another recipient; then reject a protocol transition while optimistic bytes are buffered and prevent desynchronization. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Test client and server state transitions under delayed, missing, duplicate, fragmented, and rejected expectations without deadlock.

Evidence target: Defend the transferred model · Lab Expect Deadlock Proof.
steps.why: Retrieving Expect Deadlock Proof through the concrete expectation and early-response coordination state machine case exposes causal gaps that recognition alone can hide. For Defend the transferred model · Lab Expect Deadlock Proof, keep Expect Deadlock Proof connected to executable Go behavior evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Test client and server state transitions under delayed, missing, duplicate, fragmented, and rejected expectations without deadlock.

hints: For Defend the transferred model · Lab Expect Deadlock Proof, name the types, ownership, and stop path, Expect Deadlock Proof precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Defend the transferred model · Lab Expect Deadlock Proof, trace this concrete input and predict values, errors, blocking, and output before reviewing it: A lab protocol team handles case 7485 in a canary edge service advertising HTTP/3 with bounded fallback. Build deterministic Go evidence to coordinate 100-continue and bounded early rejection without deadlock or unsafe reuse; preserve this operating constraint: no malformed framing may be normalized and forwarded to another recipient; then reject a protocol transition while optimistic bytes are buffered and prevent desynchronization. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Test client and server state transitions under delayed, missing, duplicate, fragmented, and rejected expectations without deadlock.

For Defend the transferred model · Lab Expect Deadlock Proof, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, failure, race, or toolchain evidence: A happy-path continue exchange proves both peers avoid coordination deadlocks.
